paragraphs.body: to bite at the hand that feeds

annotations.comment: All lower-case is a mixed bag, in my opinion.

paragraphs.body: Your mother rarely lets you out of her sight. Even now, as she hangs laundry, she’s brought you along, telling you to stay close. But here you are, one foot in the woods and one foot out, the shadows deeper just ahead. You shouldn’t be this far back, but there’s something there. Someone.

Deeper in, behind a bush, you catch a glimpse of a pale little face. It peeks up, giggling and waving you over, then vanishes just as quickly. You glance around, sure that you and your brothers are the only children here. You’re a good child—your mother always says so. You wouldn’t want to make her worry. But… you’re also a lonely child. It’s just you, your brothers, and the endless trees, and neither of them make for very good company. The trees don’t share your games. Your brothers don’t either.

annotations.text: You’re a good child—your mother always says so
annotations.comment: This opening is captivating! I did wonder if the mother things the MC is both good and in need of constant oversight.

paragraphs.body: So when you see that pale little face again, peeking up from behind the bush and laughing like they’re having the time of their life, something stirs in your chest. You’ve never seen anyone else your size out here—not once. You’re confused, of course, but more than that, you’re excited. Just this once, you’d love to make a new friend.

annotations.comment: Just an FYI that I pictured something smaller than childsize here, like a fairy.

paragraphs.body: You step into the forest without a backward glance. When you reach the bush, they’re nowhere to be found. And then you spot them, hiding behind a tree just a little further in. They giggle again, waving you over. A game of chase, then. You’re very fast—faster than your brothers, even. Smiling, you run after them. The game goes on and on, until you’re further into the woods than you’ve ever been. You’re well and truly lost, and your friend is nowhere to be seen.

annotations.comment: I’d be interested to know if there’s some enchantment going on here or if it is the desire to play.

paragraphs.body: “Hello?” you call, spinning in circles. The trees seem to close in around you, but then—a rustle. You turn and jump. There are two of them now, not one. Both are as pale as moonlight and no taller than you. You smile. “You both look funny.”

They grin back, showing thin lips and white, white teeth. You laugh, and they laugh with you, their strange voices echoing in the trees. Such strange friends you’ve made, with their strange looks and stranger laughs. They reach for your hands, their skin cool and pale against your own deep brown, as if you were carved from earth and them from starlight. They tug, pulling you backward, their eyes never leaving yours. They’re leading you somewhere deeper, toward a place where the trees grow so close there’s barely any sunlight.

annotations.text: Such strange friends you’ve made, with their strange looks and stranger laughs
annotations.comment: This sentence didn’t do anything for the piece.
I wonder if the MC is at all scared of their paleness.

paragraphs.body: Their grip tightens. You tell them you might get in trouble, but they just stare, wide smiles unchanged.
Then you hear it.
“Wisteria! This isn’t funny.” Your eldest brother’s voice. “Come back before Father does. You know how he feels about the woods!”

annotations.comment: I always have trouble believing that people are called “Father” and “Mother.” Feels so formal.

paragraphs.body: At the sound of his voice, your friends’ faces fall. Their smiles twist into scowls, and for the first time, you feel something close to fear. Not for you, but for your brother.
“Don’t hurt him,” you say, your voice firm. They glance at each other, then back at you, before slowly dropping your hands.

annotations.comment: I love this scene — it’s rich and interesting.

paragraphs.body: They let you go, and as you walk back toward the sound of your brother’s voice, you turn to look over your shoulder. They’re still there, watching, smiling, waving. You hesitate, then wave back, before running to your brother.

It's easy to separate the faes from the humans. The fairies themselves are normal enough at first glance, but when stared at a little longer, there's always something odd in their appearance that makes her uneasy. They're trying to take on human form but it’s wrong. Eyes too deep or too tall, face too flat, nose sinking in instead of protruding. They try to mimic humanity, but the longer you stare at some of them, it seems their faces begin to drip away, the structure of them no longer holds together. Maybe that's why she's less apt to join in on the festivities as everyone celebrates. That and the fact that she still doesn't know how she ended up here.

annotations.comment: The switch to third person threw me off.

paragraphs.body: She can’t remember the last night. Or has it been longer? She remembers falling asleep in her bed and then waking to music so enchanting, she didn’t even question why she was standing at the treeline. After that, it’s a blur until she’s waking up here, on a pile of dead leaves surrounded by dancing figures and fungi. Instead of enjoying the party, she holds on tight to her skepticism. Why is she here? Though the reason eludes her, she's more than sure he has something to do with it.

annotations.comment: I need some help understanding whether this person is the same as the “you” in the first section.
Now I am very confused, because this is a different situation than in the first section.

paragraphs.body: She can tell he's someone important with the way everyone flocks to him, hanging off his every word like his voice alone is sacred. No, she decides, it’s more than that. He looks different than the rest of them. Sharp teeth that shine white under the moon's gaze whenever he smiles, fingernails he uses in place of forks. Despite her manners, she stares as he quite literally shimmers and shines under the fire's light. Instead of skin like tree bark or the surface of the moon, he is tanned like the neighbor boy who would toil away under the hot sun. He’s broad-shouldered with bouncing hair the color of straw that curls behind pointed ears and down the slender slope of his elegant nape. He wears only a crown fitted with two strong, bending deer antlers like the bare branches of a grand tree and a piece of cloth that hangs low on his hips, of which she’s grateful. Most, if not all, fairies choose to go bare. He looks so nearly human that she isn’t certain she can handle seeing that much of him. As he dances with the others to the beat of an invisible drum, she realizes that it isn't cloth at all, but instead very many flowers, stems, and pieces of grass that were braided into a covering. As he spins, something catches the light. So thin and delicate, that she hadn’t noticed before, but now that she does, it’s impossible not to see them. Wings, two pairs, one above the other like a dragonfly. They move individually, large enough for his stature, but still so thin that she can’t imagine how they carry him.

annotations.comment: It’s a great description. I’m starting to suspect that this is more likely the opening to a novel than a short story.
annotations.context_left: She can tell he's someone important with the way everyone flocks to
annotations.context_right: , hanging off his every word like his voice alone is sacred. No, she decides, it’s more than that. H

paragraphs.body: Blue eyes brush over her occasionally, before looking away whenever someone else catches his attention, but they don’t stray from her for long. He certainly has something to do with her being there if that's anything to go off of. But she doesn’t spend much time worrying about him. She’s too busy fending off the advances of the other fairies. Many of them approach her, staring at her with impish eyes as they ask for a dance. But she knows better than to entertain their whims. She turns them down as respectfully as she can.

annotations.text: But she knows better than to entertain their whims
annotations.comment: What does she know, and how does she know it? Is there a purpose to keeping this information from us?

paragraphs.body: They come in all shapes and sizes, some so small they’re hardly more than giggling twinkling dots that fly around her and some so tall and wirey they could have easily been mistaken for another tree in the clearing if they stood still enough. They are of nature, that much is clear. Their wings, ranging from butterflies to eagles to bats, pour from their backs and even take the place of some of their arms. Some of their limbs look broken, legs bent inward, ankles curled out, fingers bent in the wrong direction. Backward like they’re twisted all the way around. All the wrong parts of humans and animals cut and stitched onto their wretched canvases.

annotations.comment: It’s clear that you really enjoy describing the world of the fairies (and more power to you, it seems lovely). However, if this is going to work as a story, we need a plot, not just description. What’s going to happen in this story? Who is the main character? What do they want?

paragraphs.body: Fending them off each time they ask is almost like swatting away gnats, flies, and mosquitoes. And saying they ask is quite the stretch. Every word sounds like wind trickling through chimes, church bells shrunken down to boom along her eardrums, a sharpened blade cutting through the air. She doesn’t understand them—she shouldn’t be able to. Regardless, she does. Each tug on her tight curls is met with, “I’m so very tired.” Each yank on her gown, “I have two left feet.” Each pull on her fingers, “There’s so many of you, how could I possibly dance with you all?”

Anything but no. Because no is rude. No is unkind. No isn't a good enough reason to turn them away. Not when the chance of turning them down, rejecting them, making them anything other than pleased with her can lead to… Well, she doesn't want to know what it'll lead to. They've been kind enough to her, haven't made her dance when they surely could, haven’t pulled her hair too hard, haven’t made her bleed. Other than the cold and the hunger and the fog in her mind, she feels about the same as when she went to bed. She can’t entirely blame them for not being accommodating hosts. They are not of this world, creatures she’d never assume to understand. They do not eat because of hunger, do not light fires to chase away the cold. For them, it’s the same as stumping your toe and saying ow even if it doesn’t hurt.

annotations.comment: Why not just dance with them? I don’t understand.

paragraphs.body: The fae had been in her grandmother’s stories—creatures who stole infants and replaced them with changelings, who made humans dance until their feet bled. But remembering the rules now, as the festivities swirl around her, is like chasing shadows. What had Grandmother Luna told her?
Never say ‘thank you’, never give them your full name, and never accept their gifts.

annotations.comment: Great, I like this method of getting this information across. Consider moving this up in teh story — it belongs next to the first time we understand she’s telling them no.

paragraphs.body: What else is there? As Wisteria watches the fae dance around the fire, fragments of grandmother’s warnings flicker in the back of her mind, but they come in pieces, fractured and hard to grasp. Something about salt in the windows… rusted nails…? She remembers grandmother’s hands, frail but insistent as they press these things into the corners of her home. Why?

“The fae…” grandmother had whispered once, but the memory feels distant, muffled under layers of fog. What had she said? Something about…memories? Debts? Wisteria’s thoughts sift through her fingers like clumps of dirt. The words echo, but they’re incomplete, broken. She hadn’t understood then—she doesn’t understand now—but as the fae dance, shimmering and flickering at the edges of her vision, she feels a weight pressing down on her chest. A deep sense of…something. Owed?
Her stomach knots. Why am I here?

annotations.text: stomach knots. Why am I here?
annotations.comment: Can we learn more about how she ended up here? (It may be that that the first section is how she ended up here, but that’s not clear yet)

paragraphs.body: A laugh breaks through the rising noise in her mind, one of the fae spinning close to her, all sharp teeth and bright eyes. She tries to focus, to pull the memory closer, but it dances away as easily as the creatures circling the fire.
They always…collect… Her grandmother’s voice comes back, faint as a whisper on the wind.

Collect what? She can’t be sure. She can’t be sure of much of anything anymore. She has no idea how long she’s been here. The moon hasn’t moved from its place in the sky and she hasn’t felt the need to sleep, but her stomach cramps up with the need to eat. She can’t work up the courage to ask for food, and she can’t even stand up without feeling nauseous with hunger. She hunches over her gurgling stomach, knees pulled up to her chest, and pushes her back further into the base of the tree she’s taking refuge at.

She’s bitter at how easily everyone seems to be enjoying themselves, especially him. He bites into something that loosely resembles a fruit before throwing his head back in delighted laughter. There he is indulging himself in foods that look vaguely familiar while her stomach eats itself. Drops of the fruit dot his mouth, its juice, bloody like a fresh kill, trails down his fingers to drip at his wrist to the ground. Down his chin, his neck, and his chest. How wasteful. She’s shocked at how easily she imagines following those rivulets with her tongue. The hunger—and perhaps his magic—must be to blame.
And almost as if he’s summoned by her scampering thoughts—
“Wherefore art thou so forlorn of countenance?”
She gasps at the sudden appearance of a jovial voice. Such a large being should not be able to move so silently.

"I am but," she tucks herself further into the tree as he towers over her. He’s big, but not absurdly so. Still, there’s something about him—intimidating, yes, but more than that, something she can’t quite name. "a bit under the weather."

annotations.comment: Is this the guy eating the fruit or someone else?
I wondered why she always slightly lies to them.

paragraphs.body: "There is no cause for sorrow when friends surround thee," His voice is smooth, almost too warm, as he sweeps a grand arm over the revelries. And though his words are strange to her ears, at least he speaks. "Especially as our guest of honor." His teeth gleam sharper up close, and a shiver crawls down her spine. There’s a tension in the air, but she can’t tell if it’s from fear—or something else entirely. His words slide into her mind through a haze, but once they settle, the weight of them hits her.

“Wait, what? Guest of honor?” She struggles to stand, but before she can, he sits beside her with a heavy thunk. She tries to move away, but he’s magnetic, the heat of his bare skin seeping through her thin nightgown like a siren’s song.

annotations.comment: Okay, so not the same as the first section?

paragraphs.body: "Indeed! I have awaited this moment to speak with thee for a long…long time. It feels like years." He tilts his head, lowering himself to catch her eye. It’s not a crown, she realizes. Those antlers are sprouting from his thick, blond hair, catching the moonlight. His piercing gaze, so close now, leaves her feeling exposed, flayed open beneath his scrutiny, vulnerable as he openly admires her.

The fae—they mimic humans, but they don’t understand them. They laugh when someone pricks a finger, but they’d laugh just as easily if they pushed someone off a roof. Even though he is so close to human, the social nuances are lost on him. He leans in closer, his gaze unblinking as she forces a nervous smile. His wings twitch faintly on either side of him, casting thin shadows in the firelight.

annotations.comment: I love this depiction of a character almost human but not quite.

paragraphs.body: “How strange,” he breathes, his voice thick with intrigue, perhaps even awe. His nail brushes against her front teeth, cold and sharp, tapping gently as if testing their fragility. “To possess teeth so flat. We shall have to see to getting that straightened out.” He murmurs low enough that she can pretend she never heard it at all. Behind the curve of his lips, she can just make out the gleam of his own teeth—knife-like, serrated. Teeth like those are meant to tear at more than the mere skin of fruit.

“What have I done that is worthy of so much,” she returns to the original topic at hand, floundering for a word that could capture the debauchery swirling around her while still being tactful. "...honoring?"
His brow furrows, head tilting in a way that reminds her of a dog—no, not a dog. A giant hound with razor-sharp teeth.

"Why dost thou think we must possess a reason to celebrate thee?" His voice is light, playful, as though her confusion amuses him. "Just thy very existence is a feat!" He throws his head back, laughing, the sound sharp and unsettling—more like the chittering of a cicada than anything she’s ever heard.

Her fingers twitch, her body urging her to argue, to scream, to demand the truth. Why did he take her? What did he want? She feels the heat of anger rising in her chest, the urge to lash out—perhaps with words that would have had her poor father rolling in his grave. But before she can do any of that, another memory flickers through the haze. A rule grandmother had drilled into her, over and over again.

annotations.comment: I love the mystery here, but we’re halfway through the story and I don’t know how much longer you can sort of hide whatever is going on. At some point, the problem here needs to emerge.

paragraphs.body: Be very, very polite.

"What should I call you, then? I think I’m due the name of my admirer." The sharpness in her tone makes her wince inwardly almost as soon as the words leave her lips. No matter her frustration, she knows she must keep her manners, especially with someone as important as him. He scratches at the back of his head, an oddly human gesture, before leaning closer to her in a way that really should have made her nervous. But as he draws nearer, she notices that the other fae keep their distance, giving her a wide berth, and she finds a strange comfort in his proximity.

annotations.comment: Makes more sense for her to be slightly rude and then remember what her grandmother said.
annotations.context_right: n? I think I’m due the name of my admirer." The sharpness in her tone makes her wince inwardly almos

paragraphs.body: “’Tis not within my bounds to speak it, yet,” he drags the word out, a twinkle of mischief in his eyes, “I grant thee leave—thou mayst name me Soren.” Wisteria blinks, her heart pounding. Did he just say…‘Soren’? Or was it something else? All of his words tangle in her mind, half-familiar yet strange enough that she isn’t sure if she has understood him correctly.

annotations.comment: I think you’re slightly overdoing the olde timey language. Maybe just “name me Soren” here?
Why does this bother her?

paragraphs.body: “I know full well who thou art, Wisteria.” His chuckle is low this time, a rich drawl that slides through the air like silk. “This celebration is in thy name, after all.”

annotations.comment: He may know — but we do not!

paragraphs.body: She blinks. And then blinks again. It takes her a moment to realize the implications of what he has said. Her name, that is. He said her name. Not a pet name, not a nickname, but her true name—the one her mother whispered over her when she was born, the one that should be kept safe. Names are power, grandmother had warned her once. In the hands of the fae, your name is your soul, your will. And she had never told him hers. Fear blooms cold in her chest. Her heartbeat starts sluggish but quickly picks up speed and power, beating at her sternum like a woodpecker trapped in the cage of her ribs.

annotations.comment: I’m confused by this because her brother openly called her by this name in the earlier section?

paragraphs.body: “How did you—?”
“Have thy name?” He finishes for her, and she freezes like some sort of prey animal. Have her name. What strange wording. Not know it, but have it.

“That, my dear Wisteria, is a tale for another day. Far too long and tedious to tell at a celebration such as this. But thou dost agree—my having thy name means it’s only fair I should tell thee mine.” There has to be a catch; there is always a catch with faeries. They do not do anything out of the goodness of their hearts, for there is no goodness to speak of. What would he know of fairness?
“...I suppose I cannot argue against it, but—”
“But thou dost agree, dost thou not.” It is not a question but a command cloaked in velvet. Something deep within her recoils. Her lips betray her and she answers as he prods her to.

“I…agree.” Satisfaction bleeds through his eyes as he smiles down at her, and the world seems to close in around her as if the very trees have borne witness to a binding far deeper than words. Around her, the fae dance faster, their movements jerky and inhuman, their feet barely touching the ground. The drums echo in her skull, a primal rhythm that quickens her pulse. Laughter—high and wild—rings out, and every so often, she catches glimpses of sharp teeth and glowing eyes watching her with too much interest.

annotations.comment: Uh-oh.

paragraphs.body: “I have given thee my name, and now, by thine own word, thou hast agreed it is fair. A small gift for a soul as bright as thine,” He—Soren purrs, grinning like a cat that has trapped a mouse. “Thou art cold.” He says this so suddenly that she is taken aback. Then, all at once, the cold creeps back in as if it had been waiting. Every inch of her is freezing cold, the night’s frigid fingers dancing up and down her skin like spiders.

annotations.comment: I need to better understand this moment — what has changed?
annotations.context_left: “I have given thee my name, and now, by
annotations.context_right: own word, thou hast agreed it is fair. A small gift for a soul as bright as thine,” He—Soren purrs,

paragraphs.body: She is cold. She is so very cold. She shivers, so cold that even the silk of her gown has lost any heat, rubbing against her like sheets of ice.
“Poor thing.”
He shakes his head, sucking his teeth three times in quick succession. “Such an easy fix, in retrospect, hm? Why not draw nearer to the fire?”
How can she explain to him in a way he would understand that moving at all is nearly impossible?

“Oh! I think I know. Is it pity thou dost seek? For us to look upon thee and feel sorrow? Is that it? Didst thou desire my attention so greatly that thou wouldst endure the freezing clutches of death for it?” He asks, but his voice does not match his words, nor his expression. While his words imply concern, his voice is amused—entertained, it seems, by her discomfort. The air around him feels almost condescending now, yet still somehow kind.

annotations.comment: Notice how the passage lengthens when formatted correctly. Again, I feel shut out here. I don’t understand what’s happening.

paragraphs.body: Confused, she stares at him, her mouth opening and closing at the accusation. The shift is too sudden, her frozen brain unable to keep pace.  He watches, observing her closely, pointed ears twitching in time with his wings. His bright eyes dart from her mouth to her furrowed brow, then to her hunched frame, and back again.
“N–no, I’d—I’d never.” She sputters, “Why…why would I do that?”

“Nay, nay, of course not.” He shakes his head, frowning dramatically, the ends of his mouth pulling down in a cruel curve as he nods along to what she says. Idly, he places the stems of flowers in her hair, undoubtedly standing out among the black coils. “

Well. If thou refusest the fire’s warmth, may I have thy hand? To take it, fair Wisteria, is to seal thy fate to mine—only then might I warm thee, and warm thee forevermore, as is proper between us.” Her mind stutters a bit at the queer wording of his statement, but then she thinks about it. He’s like a furnace beside her, heated steel wrapped in golden skin. The distance between her and the fire seems to grow, further than she remembers it ever being. She sighs, holding out what feels like frostbitten fingers.
“Yes, you may…please.”

He’s grinning, dimples deep, nose scrunched while his eyes shine like she gave him all he’s ever wanted instead of her hands. He moves faster than she can blink, curving around her in a way that should’ve felt relieving but brings to mind a snake twisting up a tree. She doesn’t warm up like normal. She’s shared a bed with her siblings during the winter months, she knows how it feels to be warmed with body heat. This is not that. This feels like the heat of illness, the gradual but rapid build-up of an internal fever that makes you sweat…But she doesn’t hate it, she even relaxes into it. It’s almost pleasant, settling into a sickness.

annotations.comment: Is she enchanted?

paragraphs.body: In a moment that could stretch between a second and a millennium, she wakes in his arms with a start when he reaches up to pluck a low-hanging fruit from a branch. Odd, considering she thought this tree was bare. In fact, she’s certain it was bare. That’s one of the main reasons her stomach has remained empty.

“Thou hadst not inquired, yet for this…union, there is the ancient rite—Sióg Dorcha Póga an T-amadán—‘tis the dark fairy’s kiss to the fool, as it were, but no longer, for thou art beyond the bounds of folly. We must bite of this fruit in twain, at once.” Everything after that seems distant, unheard, as he raises the strange fruit he plucked from the tree. It looks like a strawberry but…wrong. She wrinkles her nose at the smell—something akin to rot, entwined with the faint sweetness of her grandmother's sugar-marinated fruit cakes. Seedless, pale yellow, and unsettling. And then, she understands: here is the catch. To accept any gift from the fae is to owe them.

annotations.comment: I can’t quite tell if the narrator is referring tot his gift or the gift of the name, or what? I think you need to work on getting this information out to the reader, but also make the sequence of events more clear to us as well. It’s a bit of a muddle, what’s happening and what those events are causing.

paragraphs.body: “’Tis much like a kiss,” he says, voice as soft as freshly washed sheets left to dry, but the words are not so simple.

"No!" The commotion around her goes deathly still. Even she’s surprised by her outburst. His smile vanishes and any ounce of eager friendliness drips off of Soren’s face like syrup as if it had never been there. The abrupt disappearance of something she hadn't even realized was there sets her on edge. So does the encroaching cold. For a moment, he stares at her with empty eyes, the heat that once radiated from him is replaced by a chill that steals the breath from her lungs.

annotations.comment: Good, I’m glad she fought this!

paragraphs.body: “Nay? Dost thou dare to deny me?” The probing look in his eyes is almost sharper than his teeth as he studies her with frigid regard and she wonders where the friendly fairy went. "I knew humans were careless, but to insult me on my land?" His sharp nails puncture the fruit and she watches as the juice runs down his hand in rivulets. Murmurs of disapproval all around her and not for the first time that night, she’s reminded of the position she’s in.

annotations.text: reminded of the position she’s in.
annotations.comment: We haven’t seen her try to get away.

paragraphs.body: "I–I meant no disrespect—"

“Shh, shh. I know, I know.” His demeanor shifts with the swiftness of a lightning strike, rocking her back and forth as he strokes her hair, much like her grandmother once did to calm her as a girl. “That is why I’ll permit it to pass.” He leans close, his breath warm against her ear as he lowers his voice as if sharing a secret. “But I cannot promise the others will be as understanding if thou dost not take a bite.” Each whispered word is emphasized as he points a thumb behind his back. She glances around and sure enough, finds an avid crowd watching them. They giggle behind their hands, their disfigured faces lit with delight, muttering among themselves as if they know a joke she’s not in on. She’ll have to comply with their whims, their leniency ends here.
"If I eat this, do I get to go home?"
“If thou dost partake, thou mayest do as thou wilt.” She must place her trust in him; fairies never lie.

annotations.text: She must place her trust in him; fairies never lie.
annotations.comment: Hoo, boy. I wonder if you can find a way earlier to show that they don’t lie?

paragraphs.body: The drums thump louder, and the pace the fae dance becomes feverish. It echoes between her ears and slides over the grooves of her brain and if she listens carefully, she can hear faint voices intertwining and overlapping. Chimes and bells and slashes and whispers. She feels her heartbeat in her tongue. It matches the pace of the drums. Her vision swims and sharpens as he places the fruit between his lips. She’s never been so hungry in her life; she can almost feel her stomach brush against her spine with every breath. One bite can’t possibly hurt. Can it?

When she finally leans forward to take a bite of the fruit, she perceives three things in quick succession: how soft Soren’s lips feel against hers, the length of his eyelashes as they flutter against his cheeks, and the taste of the fruit as she bites into it. There are no words in any tongue known to man that could adequately describe it. Yet everything in her body agrees on one truth: she craves more. She barely notices the warm hands grabbing at her hips and pulling her onto his muscled thighs. Lights flash behind her eyelids as her fingers tangle in his silken hair. She traces her tongue over the roof of his mouth, chasing the fruit, nipping herself on his sharp teeth. Even the bitter taste of blood can’t overshadow the ambrosial flavor.

Not finding any more in his mouth, she licks at the juice that trails down his neck all the while Soren shakes with laughter. Blearily, she melts into his embrace, desperately chasing the mingled taste of his mouth and the fruit. She was a fool to ever want to leave. Cheers erupt around them—animalistic cries of glee, wings flapping, hooves stomping, trees shaking. They cheer for her, for them. Her last coherent thought is a rule, one she remembers far too late.
Never, under any circumstances, eat the food of the fae.

annotations.comment: This feels a little flat, since we were already reminded. We watch her do it, knowing she shouldn’t. It’s beautifully written but doesn’t have much suspense.

paragraphs.body: Some deals are made in haste, and only later do you realize what you’ve truly given away. If she had known then what she knows now, Luna never would have made that deal. She thought she could outsmart them. Thought herself clever enough to trick them. When she asked them for help winning over her now late husband, the trade they offered made her gawk. They’d make sure he loved her instead of his then-intended, and in return, all they asked for was the first female offspring.

annotations.comment: Who is this?

paragraphs.body: “Thou canst always have another, after all.”

The fae are odd that way, not understanding just how uneven and unbalanced their trades were. But she thought she could handle it. Get her husband and keep her child. A friend of hers had a boy, only three winters. So, freshly after giving birth, she arranged a marriage between him and her little girl. Had it written in paper, a deal that couldn’t be broken by any outside party. And as soon as she could, she had her daughter baptized. The fae were more than angry, they were livid. Killed their crops, their animals, brought about a plague—and then it stopped. She thought her daughter had escaped the danger until her last child, her first daughter.

annotations.comment: I’m so confused.

paragraphs.body: Wisteria. Such a beautiful little girl. She tried to warn them, tell her daughter and son-in-law what they needed to do to keep her safe. He didn’t believe it, so neither did she. It was up to Luna. She left salt in Wisteria’s window, around her crib, at the door. Left rusted metal under the crib. She protected her. Taught her the rules.

annotations.comment: Is the grandmother still alive?

paragraphs.body: Her son-in-law was an authoritative man, a mean one. He never hit her daughter, never hit his kids. But he was rough with them, salt over sugar. He simply didn't have the disposition for children. Left the child-rearing for the women until the children needed to be punished. The children feared him, stopped laughing when he entered a room, and preferred to play outside whenever he was home. It was one of these days that something happened. Wisteria wandered near the woods, and he caught her before she crossed the treeline. He picked her up by her arm and dragged her to the porch, shaking her and yelling at her as she sobbed. But he did it in full view of the woods.
He died the same month.

annotations.comment: Oh, I thought it was her brother who found her. I would consider putting this section second — it really helps me understand and contextualize the other two sections.

paragraphs.body: Her daughter still didn’t believe her, hadn’t had Wisteria baptized but agreed to make sure the boys kept a close eye on their sister. As Wisteria grew into a woman, coming of age, she began to sleepwalk. Luna only got weaker with age, and the fae knew it. In hindsight, she would have found a different way, would have…would have….

She’s foggy on her deathbed. Her children and grandchildren gathered around her. To the left, on the far side of the room in the only window is a face. Glittering skin, and antlers. The king himself watches with his big blue eyes as Luna takes her last breath. She tries to say something, to warn, but a weight presses down on her chest, silencing her. Her ribs creak like rotten wooden branches, bending and splintering under the relentless push of a cold wind. There’s no space left in the fragile cage of her chest to gasp—every breath feels like it might splinter her entirely. All she can do is stare at him as he stares at her. Faintly, she sees dots like fireflies floating around her, but no one else reacts.

The king smiles, his sharp teeth glinting in the dim light, and Luna’s breath comes quicker, her chest tightening. The weight pressing down on her is unbearable now—like cold iron pushing the very life from her lungs.

I thought I could protect her, she thinks, her mind frantic, but her body too weak to move. She can see his blue eyes glittering at the edge of the room, his antlers casting long shadows across the walls. The drums grow louder, each beat striking her bones like a hammer on an anvil. She tries to speak—tries so desperately to warn her daughter—but the words die on her tongue, crushed by the weight of him, of the deal, of all the years spent hoping, bargaining, and running from the suffocating, unyeilding truth: the fae always collect.
His smile widens, and she hears the chimes and bells, the eerie giggles, the music that will soon carry her granddaughter away. And then…
Nothing.

annotations.comment: Nova, this is a gripping and ambitious story. I really am not a fae person at all, but I enjoyed this. I thought you did a good job of capturing the world in the story. I do think you’ve lingered over description too much at first and then rushed a bit to get in the story at the end. But more importantly, each section doesn’t quite feel like it’s working yet. The last section feels like it might need to be the second (and I would encourage you to think about some subtitles, maybe their names or a year?) and then in the final section, about Wisteria, I would like you to think about how it can be more than a foregone conclusion that she will be taken. You have a lot of great potential in there, where she seems to be trying to remember what her grandmother taught her — I wonder what happens if she really thinks she’s fighting back and remembering correctly but then gets caught anyway? Or maybe she does get away, thanks to her grandmother. Lots of good stuff here. Think about how to let the reader in. Happy revising!
